title: Writing Text Files
summary: Create or overwrite text files by opening paths in write or append mode and writing text that you can verify with printed output.

sketchBlocks.title: Write mode creates or replaces text
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When you open a path with write mode (`"w"`), Python prepares that path for new text. If the path already has text, write mode replaces the old contents.

```python
with open("note.txt", "w") as file:
    file.write("Hello\n")

with open("note.txt") as file:
    print(file.read())
```

Step by step:

- `open("note.txt", "w")` opens the path for writing.
- `file.write("Hello\n")` puts text into the file.
- Opening the same path again in read mode lets you check what was saved.
- `print(file.read())` sends the saved text to the output panel.

In this example, the output is:

```python
Hello
```

Try it yourself: change `"Hello\n"` to a different message, click Run, and check the new output.

sketchBlocks.title: Append mode adds to the end
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Append mode (`"a"`) keeps the existing text and adds new text at the end.

```python
with open("log.txt", "w") as file:
    file.write("Start\n")

with open("log.txt", "a") as file:
    file.write("Next\n")

with open("log.txt") as file:
    print(file.read())
```

Line by line:

- The first `with open(..., "w")` creates the starting text.
- The second `with open(..., "a")` adds another line without removing `Start`.
- Reading the file back shows both lines.

The output becomes:

```python
Start
Next
```

Your turn: change the second written line, run the code, and predict what the full file text will be before you check the output panel.

sketchBlocks.title: Use newline characters and verify what you wrote
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When writing several lines, `\n` starts a new line. It is also helpful to reopen the file and print its contents so you can verify the result.

```python
with open("tasks.txt", "w") as file:
    file.write("Buy milk\nWash dishes\n")

with open("tasks.txt") as file:
    saved_text = file.read()
    print(saved_text)
```

What this does:

- `Buy milk\n` writes one line and moves to the next line.
- `Wash dishes\n` writes the second line.
- `file.read()` gets the full saved text.
- Printing `saved_text` lets you confirm the file contents in the output panel.

Without `\n`, both tasks would appear on the same line.

Try this: add a third task to the string, click Run, and check whether each task appears on its own line.

quizDraft.title: Choose the mode that replaces old text
quizDraft.prompt: Which file mode should you use when you want to overwrite any existing text in a file with new text?
quizDraft.hint: Think about the mode used to create fresh contents from the start of the file.

help.concept: Write mode starts the file contents over, while append mode keeps existing contents and adds more text at the end.
help.hint_1: Compare what `"w"` does to existing text with what `"a"` does.
help.hint_2: Choose the mode that replaces old contents instead of preserving them.

quizDraft.title: Best way to confirm saved text
quizDraft.prompt: After writing text to a file in this lesson, what is the best way to make the result visible in the output panel?
options: Reopen the file and print its contents
quizDraft.hint: Think about reopening the path and showing the saved contents.

help.concept: File-writing exercises should produce observable output, so a common pattern is to reopen the file and print what was written.
help.hint_1: Writing changes the file, but the output panel only shows text you print.
help.hint_2: Choose the option that reads the saved text back and displays it.

quizDraft.title: Facts about append mode
quizDraft.prompt: Choose all true statements about opening a file with append mode (`"a"`).
options: It adds new text at the end of the file.
It always removes everything already in the file.
It can be used to add another line to a log.
quizDraft.hint: Append mode keeps old text and adds new text after it.

help.concept: Append mode writes at the end of existing contents instead of clearing the file first.
help.hint_1: Look for statements that describe keeping existing text.
help.hint_2: Reject any option that says append mode erases the file before writing.

quizDraft.title: Useful patterns when writing text files
quizDraft.prompt: Choose all practices that fit this topic's file-writing workflow.
options: Use `with open(...)` so the file is handled cleanly.
Use `\n` when you want the next text on a new line.
Print the saved contents after writing so the result is visible.
Change the notes panel before writing.
quizDraft.hint: Think about writing text, then making the result observable.

help.concept: Beginner file-writing programs often use `with open(...)` for safe access, newline characters for line breaks, and printed verification after writing.
help.hint_1: Look for patterns involving `with open`, `\n`, and printing saved text.
help.hint_2: Avoid choices unrelated to writing or checking file contents.

quizDraft.title: Order the write-and-check steps
quizDraft.prompt: Put these steps in the correct order for a simple program that writes text and then verifies it.
tokens: Open the same path for reading
correctOrder: Open the same path for reading
quizDraft.hint: Write first, then reopen and display the saved text.

help.concept: A common workflow is open in write mode, write text, reopen in read mode, then print the contents.
help.hint_1: The file must be opened for writing before `write()` can happen.
help.hint_2: Printing comes after reading the saved contents back.

quizDraft.title: Order the append workflow
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange these actions to show how a program can create a starting log entry, append another entry, and then display the full log.
tokens: Open the log with mode "w" and write the first line
Open the log with mode "a" and add another line
correctOrder: Open the log with mode "w" and write the first line
Open the log with mode "a" and add another line
quizDraft.hint: The first write creates the starting text before append adds more.

help.concept: To demonstrate append mode clearly, first create initial contents, then append new text, then read the whole file back.
help.hint_1: You need some starting text before the append step makes sense.
help.hint_2: Reading and printing the full log should happen after both writes are finished.

quizDraft.title: Fill the write mode
quizDraft.prompt: Complete the mode so this code opens the file for overwriting text.
quizDraft.hint: Use the mode that replaces old contents.

help.concept: The mode string for overwriting text is `"w"`.
help.hint_1: This is not append mode and not read mode.
help.hint_2: Choose the single-letter mode used to write fresh contents.

quizDraft.title: Fill the line break
quizDraft.prompt: Choose the text that starts a new line after `First line`.
quizDraft.hint: Use the escape sequence for a newline.

help.concept: In Python strings, `\n` represents a newline character.
help.hint_1: The missing part is not a space and not a tab.
help.hint_2: Pick the backslash sequence that moves the next text to a new line.

quizDraft.title: Write and show a greeting
quizDraft.prompt: Write a program that opens `greeting.txt` in write mode, writes exactly `Hello, Zoe!` to it, then reopens the file and prints its contents.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
with open("greeting.txt", "w") as file:
    file.write("Hello, Zoe!")

with open("greeting.txt") as file:
    print(file.read())
quizDraft.hint: Use one `with open(..., "w")` block to write, then another `with open(...)` block to read and print.

help.concept: A complete file-writing program often writes text first, then reads the same path back so the result appears in the output panel.
help.hint_1: After writing `Hello, Zoe!`, open `greeting.txt` again without write mode and call `read()`.
help.hint_2: Print the text you read back from the file so the checker can observe the saved result.

quizDraft.title: Overwrite a list with two lines
quizDraft.prompt: Write a program that opens `shopping.txt` in write mode, writes exactly two lines: `Bread` and `Apples`, then reopens the file and prints the full contents.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
with open("shopping.txt", "w") as file:
    file.write("Bread\nApples\n")

with open("shopping.txt") as file:
    print(file.read(), end="")
quizDraft.hint: Use `\n` between the two items so they appear on separate lines.

help.concept: Write mode replaces the file contents, and newline characters let you store multiple lines of text in one write operation.
help.hint_1: The written text should contain `Bread`, then a newline, then `Apples`.
help.hint_2: After writing, read the whole file back and print it so both lines appear in the output panel.

quizDraft.title: Append a new log entry
quizDraft.prompt: A file named `log.txt` is provided. Write a program that opens `log.txt` in append mode, adds the line `Checked sensors` with a newline, then reopens the file and prints the full contents.
quizDraft.starterCode: # Append one new line to log.txt, then print the full file contents.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
with open("log.txt", "a") as file:
    file.write("Checked sensors\n")

with open("log.txt") as file:
    print(file.read(), end="")

tests.stdout:
Start shift
Doors locked
Checked sensors

quizDraft.hint: Append should keep the existing lines and add the new line at the end.

help.concept: Append mode adds new text after the current contents instead of replacing them, so reading the file back should show both old and new lines.
help.hint_1: Open `log.txt` with mode `"a"` and write `Checked sensors\n`.
help.hint_2: Then reopen `log.txt`, read the whole file, and print it exactly as stored.

projectDraft.title: Practice writing and appending text files
